subject: English
title: Continuous Writing
outline: Layout, salutation and tone of informal letters

modules.title: Narrative essay
modules.html:
A narrative essay tells a story. It follows a clear plot: exposition → rising action → climax → falling action → resolution.

Key ingredients

  • A clear setting — time and place.
  • A small number of well-drawn characters.
  • A conflict that drives the story.
  • A definite climax — the turning point.
  • A satisfying ending that ties up the plot.

Use past tense throughout; use dialogue sparingly; describe sights, sounds and feelings to make the reader see and hear what happened.

examples.q: Give a strong opening for a narrative titled The Day I Was Bullied.
examples.a: e.g. The morning sun could not warm the fear that lay in my chest as I trudged towards the school gate that Monday. It sets time, place and mood at once.

modules.title: Descriptive essay
modules.html:
A descriptive essay paints a picture in words. It appeals to the five senses — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — so the reader can imagine the person, place or scene.

Tips

  • Choose a clear point of view (first person or observer).
  • Use vivid adjectives and strong verbs — the market bustled, not the market was busy.
  • Organise details by space (top to bottom, near to far) or by time.
  • Use figurative language: similes, metaphors, personification.

examples.q: Rewrite for stronger imagery: The market was busy and noisy.
examples.a: The market throbbed with the clang of hawkers' bells, the sizzle of frying akara, and the tide of shoppers flowing between stalls.

modules.title: Informal letter — structure and tone
modules.html:
An informal letter is written to a friend or relative. It is friendly, chatty and personal.

Layout

  1. Writer's address — top right, no name, each line separated by commas, full stop at the end.
  2. Date — one line below the address.
  3. Salutation — Dear Musa, (comma after the name).
  4. Opening paragraph — enquire after the reader's health / respond to the last letter.
  5. Body — the reason for writing, in two or three paragraphs.
  6. Closing paragraph — round off, send greetings.
  7. Complimentary close — Your friend, / Your loving cousin,
  8. Signature — first name only.

examples.q: Correct salutation for a friend called Adaeze?
examples.a: Dear Ada, — note the comma after her name.

quiz.q: A narrative essay tells:
The turning point of a story is called the:
Which tense dominates in a narrative essay?
quiz.solution: Stories usually told in the past tense.

quiz.q: A descriptive essay appeals mainly to:
Which is a sensory detail?
options: The sky was blue-grey with racing clouds.

quiz.q: In an informal letter, the writer's address is placed:
The salutation in an informal letter to your father is:
The complimentary close of an informal letter is often:
Which sentence is best for opening a story?
options: I want to tell you a story.
It happened on a chilly Monday morning.
quiz.solution: Sets time and mood at once.

quiz.q: Show, don't tell in writing means:
quiz.solution: Let the reader experience through detail.

quiz.q: A simile uses:
A metaphor:
Which is the correct order of a plot?
Which sentence uses personification?
options: The wind whispered through the trees.
quiz.solution: The wind is given human power to whisper.

quiz.q: Best synonym for gigantic in description:
An informal letter should:
options: begin with 'To whom it may concern'

quiz.q: The date in an informal letter is written:
quiz.solution: Just below the address, top right.

quiz.q: The best paragraph plan for a narrative is:
quiz.solution: Paragraphs mark stages of the story.

quiz.q: A narrative essay is usually written from a:
options: first- or third-person point of view

quiz.q: Which of these words shows the passing of time?
Which is <u>not</u> appropriate for a friendly letter?
Dialogue in a story is:
Which punctuation encloses direct speech?
Which word signals a new stage in the plot?
A vivid opening sentence in a description of a market might be:
options: The market roared with a hundred voices.

quiz.q: The last paragraph of a narrative essay should:
quiz.solution: Bring the story to a satisfying close.

quiz.q: Which of these is <u>not</u> a feature of an informal letter?
quiz.solution: Subject lines belong to formal letters and memos.

quiz.q: Which is best in a descriptive essay: The soup was ___?
options: fiery with pepper and warm with palm-oil

quiz.q: Which linking word shows sequence?
Which is a well-formed complimentary close?
The setting of a story is:
Which is a run-on sentence?
options: I read the book. It was long.
I read the book it was long.
Because I read the book, I know the story.

quiz.q: Point of view refers to:
In descriptive writing, appealing to smell might use words like:
Which is a stronger verb than walk?
An informal letter should end with:
Which sentence best builds suspense?
options: Something did not feel right about the empty street.

quiz.q: A flashback is:
options: a scene from earlier in time

quiz.q: Which is a common weakness in student narratives?
quiz.solution: Long chains of and then flatten a story.

quiz.q: Which is proper paragraphing for a 500-word narrative?
quiz.solution: Enough paragraphs to mark plot stages.

quiz.q: Which is <u>not</u> a purpose of dialogue?
quiz.solution: Morals are usually shown, not told.

quiz.q: Descriptive writing should mostly use:
Which sentence is properly capitalised?
quiz.solution: Proper nouns and sentence start capitalised.

quiz.q: Which punctuation ends a strong interjection?
An informal letter uses:
What should NOT appear in an informal letter to a friend?
options: business signature block with company logo
quiz.solution: That belongs to a formal business letter.

quiz.q: Which is a strong closing line for a story?
options: And so, at last, the little dog was safely home.

quiz.q: A protagonist is:
An antagonist is:
Which of these best describes 'mood' in writing?
quiz.solution: Mood = feeling created in the reader.
